title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- First Relapse
content: The CANDOR trial showed a 37% reduction in the risk of progression or death in a population with DKd (daratumumab, carfilzomib, and dexamethasone) compared to Kd alone. [47] Efficacy is noted to be similar between twice weekly and weekly dosages according to CANDOR and EQUULEUS trial. APOLLO trial showed 12 month PFS benefit of 52% vs 35% between Daratumumab, pomalidomide, and dexamethasone (DPd) vs pomalidomide and dexamethasone (Pd), respectively. [48]

title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
content: In cases of triple-class refractory disease, where patients have failed multiple lines of treatment, including PIs, IMiDs, and mAbs against CD38, recently approved therapies are selinexor and dexamethasone; VdT-PACE (bortezomib, dexamethasone, thalidomide, cisplatin, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, and etoposide); melflufen-dexamethasone and CAR-T-cells, idecabtagene, and belantamab mafoditin. The HORIZON study has shown that melflufen plus dexamethasone demonstrates a modicum of activity in relapsed and refractory myeloma, especially in triple-class refractory disease. [49]

title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
content: In 2022, the FDA approved teclistamab, the first bispecific T-cell engager antibody against CD38 expressed on the myeloma cells, for patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma who have failed 4 prior lines of therapy. Belantamab mafodotin is anti-BCMA humanized immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibody conjugated with monomethyl auristatin. The drug received accelerated approval in the US for patients who had progressed on at least 4 lines of therapy. It is being withdrawn from the market based on the results of randomized phase 3 trials (DREAMM-3), as it did not show clinical benefit compared to pomalidomide and dexamethasone.

title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
content: The penta-refractory disease is resistant or refractory to Anti-CD38 monoclonal antibodies, bortezomib, lenalidomide, pomalidomide, and carfilzomib. This is mainly encountered in the third- or fourth-line setting. If any of these agents have not been used before, it is reasonable to use that agent at any time. Patients with the penta-refractory disease have options of BMCA-targeted therapy (Ida-Cel, Cilta-Cel, or teclistamab); alkylator-based combination (VDT-PACE, DCEP, bendamustine); or combination regimens incorporating selinexor, venetoclax (only in cases with t(11;14) or panobinostat). Clinical trials or investigational agents are also encouraged.

title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
content: Although newer anti-MM agents have significant benefits in treating RRMM, they are not free of adverse effects. Every drug has its unique adverse effect profile and needs to be considered while treating the patient for prompt identification and management. Sometimes the adverse effects are so severe that treatment must be changed or temporarily discontinued. [59] Some of the toxicities of medications being used are as follows: Neurotoxicity: CAR-T cell therapy Ocular toxicity: belantamab mafodotin Cardiotoxicity: carfilzomib, doxorubicin Gastrointestinal toxicity: selinexor, lenalidomide Nephrotoxicity: lenalidomide, zoledronic acid Immune impression: Auto-HCT, CAR-T cell therapy, dexamethasone Myelosuppression: daratumumab, carfilzomib, selinexor, CAR-T cell therapy Peripheral neuropathy: thalidomide, protease inhibitors During the course of treatment, patients are closely monitored for adverse effects and treated accordingly.

title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- First Relapse
content: Triplet regimens are the current standard of care in relapsed or refractory settings. Lenalidomide and dexamethasone serve as the backbone in commonly used triplet regimens (KRD, DRd, IRD, Elo-Rd). Four-drug regimens like DARA-KPD (Daratumumab, carfilzomib, pomalidomide, and dexamethasone) are usually used in patients with aggressive disease and progression on bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone. Triple or quadruple drug regimens are observed to be superior over the dual combination of thalidomide-dexamethasone in patients who relapsed or developed the refractory disease after auto-HCT. title: Bortezomib -- Indications
content: Bortezomib is FDA-approved for use in the initial treatment of multiple myeloma in combination with cyclophosphamide and dexamethasone. It is also FDA-approved for use in the treatment of multiple myeloma in individuals who previously responded to bortezomib and relapsed at least six months after the completion of the prior treatment. Also, it is FDA-approved for use in the treatment of mantle cell lymphoma in individuals who have received at least one prior first-line treatment. [1] [2] [3]

title: Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management
content: For high-risk transplant-ineligible patients, there are several options. One approach would be to employ the VRd regimen for 8 to 12 cycles followed by maintenance bortezomib-based therapy. Another option could include daratumumab, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone (DRd) continued until disease progression based on the MAIA trial showing improved progression-free survival and overall survival compared to Lenalidomide and Dexamethasone. [18] This may be particularly helpful in patients ineligible for bortezomib-based therapy. For standard risk transplant-ineligible patients, options include VRd for 8 to 12 cycles followed by lenalidomide maintenance, or DRd continued until disease progression. [19] [20]

content: Inhibitory proteas refers to a class of proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Ixazomib is a specific drug that belongs to this inhibitory proteas class, acting as a reversible proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Remikiren, although primarily recognized as a renin inhibitor, is also categorized within the inhibitory proteas group because it exhibits proteasome‑inhibitory activity, placing it alongside ixazomib as another member of this therapeutic class. This relationship highlights that both ixazomib and remikiren share a common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, despite their differing primary indications.

content: Multiple myeloma, classified as a plasma‑cell disorder (اضطراب خلايا البلازما), can be managed with a variety of therapeutic agents. The alkylating agent melfalan is reported to have activity against multiple myeloma, as are its related formulations melphalan flufenamide and melphalan hydrochloride. Immunomodulatory therapy with interferon alfa‑2a may also be employed in the treatment of this disease. The proteasome inhibitor ixazomib is another option that may treat multiple myeloma, providing a targeted approach to malignant plasma cells. Additionally, the bisphosphonate kyselina zoledronová (zoledronic acid) is used to address bone disease associated with multiple myeloma and may contribute to overall disease control. Together, these agents represent a multimodal strategy for treating multiple myeloma, reflecting its classification as a plasma‑cell disorder.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.
